cards.q: Define chemotherapy and explain the tumour-growth principle it exploits.
cards.lecturer: Chemotherapy is the treatment of disease using chemical substances; the ideal agent selectively kills tumour cells with minimal toxicity to normal tissues. Tumour cells do not have a faster generation time than normal cells, but tumours have more cells in the active phases of replication, so they generally grow faster. Chemotherapy exploits this difference to preferentially kill tumour cells. The doubling time is the time for a tumour to double in size, and metastases generally have faster doubling times than the primary lesion.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The definition, and the word that matters

>> Chemotherapy = treating disease with **chemical substances**, ideally **killing tumour cells while sparing normal tissue.**

The word **"ideally"** is doing work — no real drug is perfectly selective, which is why side effects exist.

---

## Step 2: The surprising fact about tumour cells

Most students assume cancer cells divide **faster**. They don't.

>> **Tumour cells do NOT have a faster generation time.** What differs is that a **bigger FRACTION of them are actively dividing** at any moment.

So the tumour grows faster **as a population**, not because each cell is quicker.

---

## Step 3: Why this matters for the drug

Chemo drugs attack **dividing cells.**

>> Since a tumour has **more cells dividing**, it takes **more damage** than normal tissue — that's the whole selective principle.

But normal **fast-dividing** tissues (**bone marrow, gut lining, hair follicles**) are also caught — which is exactly why those are the classic side effects.

---

## Step 4: Doubling time

>> **Doubling time** = time for the tumour to double in size; **metastases double faster than the primary.**

This is why metastatic disease can respond even better to chemo (more dividing cells) but also progresses faster if untreated.

---

## Putting it all together

Chemotherapy uses **chemicals to kill tumour cells selectively.** The principle: tumour cells aren't faster, but **more of them are dividing**, and chemo attacks **dividing cells** — so tumours suffer most, while fast-dividing normal tissue (marrow, gut, hair) explains the side effects. **Metastases double faster than the primary.**
cards.tie: This principle underlies every side effect and every dosing decision in chemotherapy, so it is the natural first question. Understanding that the selectivity comes from the fraction of dividing cells — not a faster cell cycle — explains both why chemo works and why it damages marrow, gut and hair.

cards.q: Describe the five mechanistic classes of chemotherapeutic agents with examples.
cards.lecturer: Alkylating agents (melphalan, ifosfamide, cyclophosphamide) cross-link DNA strands, preventing protein synthesis and cell division. Antimetabolites (methotrexate, 5-fluorouracil) inhibit metabolic processes required for DNA/RNA synthesis. Anti-tumour antibiotics (dactinomycin, mitomycin, bleomycin, adriamycin) are natural products from soil fungi. Plant alkaloids (etoposide, vinblastine, vincristine, taxol) arrest cells in metaphase by binding the microtubular protein of the mitotic spindle. Miscellaneous agents (cisplatin, carboplatin, hexamethylmelamine) do not fit the above categories.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Learn the FAMILY and its trick, not every drug

There are five families. Each has **one mechanism** — hold the mechanism and the examples attach themselves.

---

## Step 2: The two DNA-targeting families

**Alkylating agents** (cyclophosphamide, ifosfamide, melphalan)

>> They **cross-link (glue) the DNA strands** together → the cell can't separate them to copy → no division.

**Antimetabolites** (methotrexate, 5-FU)

>> They **block the building blocks** needed to make DNA/RNA — like removing the bricks so the wall can't be built.

---

## Step 3: The natural-product family

**Anti-tumour antibiotics** (bleomycin, adriamycin/doxorubicin, mitomycin, dactinomycin)

>> **Natural products from soil fungi.** (Remember: these include the toxic ones — bleomycin→lung, adriamycin→heart.)

---

## Step 4: The spindle family

**Plant alkaloids** (vincristine, vinblastine, etoposide, taxol)

>> They **arrest the cell in METAPHASE** by binding the **microtubule protein** of the mitotic spindle — the cell can't pull its chromosomes apart.

---

## Step 5: The leftovers

**Miscellaneous** — **cisplatin, carboplatin**, hexamethylmelamine.

>> The **platinums** — hugely important in gynae-oncology — sit here because they don't fit the other categories.

---

## Putting it all together

Five families, five tricks: **alkylating = cross-link DNA; antimetabolites = block DNA/RNA building blocks; anti-tumour antibiotics = fungal products; plant alkaloids = freeze the mitotic spindle in metaphase; miscellaneous = the platinums (cisplatin, carboplatin).**
cards.tie: Classifying agents by mechanism is standard exam material, and knowing which family a drug belongs to predicts both how it works and its resistance patterns. The platinums are singled out because they are the backbone of gynaecological cancer treatment.

cards.q: Explain first-order kinetics, drug toxicity, therapeutic index, and why combination chemotherapy is preferred.
cards.lecturer: Chemotherapy works by first-order kinetics, killing a constant fraction of cells rather than a constant number. Drug toxicity is unwanted effects from injury to normal cells, limiting the dose; agents are used at doses producing some toxicity to be maximally effective. Toxicity is quantified by the therapeutic index — the ratio of therapeutic dose to toxic dose. Haematologic toxicity is the most frequent side effect. By the Goldie-Coldman hypothesis, cells start sensitive but develop resistance at variable rates, so tumours are only curable if no resistant cell lines are present or develop; to minimise resistance, multiple-drug regimens are preferred to single-drug therapy.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: First-order kinetics — the reason for repeated cycles

>> Chemo kills a constant **FRACTION**, not a constant **number**, of cells.

Think about what that means: if a dose kills **99%**, then from 1,000,000 cells you go to 10,000 — never to zero in one hit.

>> **You can never kill the last cell in a single dose** → you need **repeated cycles**, each removing another fraction.

---

## Step 2: Toxicity and the therapeutic index

>> **Toxicity** = damage to normal cells; it **limits the dose** you can give.

You **deliberately** dose to some toxicity to be maximally effective.

>> **Therapeutic index** = **therapeutic dose ÷ toxic dose.** A **wider** gap = a **safer** drug.

And the toxicity to remember:

>> **Haematologic (marrow) toxicity is the MOST frequent** — because marrow is fast-dividing.

---

## Step 3: Resistance — the Goldie-Coldman idea

>> Cells start **sensitive** but **develop resistance over time.**

The implication:

>> **A tumour is only curable if no resistant clone is present or develops.**

---

## Step 4: The solution — combination therapy

>> **Use MULTIPLE drugs together, not one.**

**Why?** A single cell is unlikely to be resistant to **several different mechanisms at once.** One drug lets a resistant clone survive and take over; several drugs kill it from different angles.

---

## Putting it all together

Chemo kills a **constant fraction** (first-order), so it needs **repeated cycles.** Dose is capped by **toxicity**, measured by the **therapeutic index** (therapeutic ÷ toxic dose), with **marrow toxicity the commonest**.

Because cells **develop resistance** (Goldie-Coldman), **combination chemotherapy is preferred** — one cell rarely resists several mechanisms at once.
cards.tie: First-order kinetics explains the whole logic of cyclical dosing, and the combination-therapy rationale is one of the most important principles in oncology. The therapeutic index and marrow toxicity are directly examinable and underpin every dosing decision.

cards.q: CLINICAL: What are the indications for chemotherapy in gestational trophoblastic disease, and how is the regimen chosen?
cards.lecturer: Indications for chemotherapy in GTD: beta-hCG exceeding 20,000 IU/ml persisting 4 weeks after evacuation; rising beta-hCG levels; histological identification of choriocarcinoma; and metastasis. Patients are categorized by the WHO scoring system (age, antecedent pregnancy, beta-hCG level, interval, tumour size, site and number of metastases, prior chemotherapy). Low-risk patients (score 0-4) receive methotrexate alone (with or without folinic acid) or actinomycin D; medium-risk (5-7) receive combination chemotherapy such as EMA-CO (etoposide, methotrexate, actinomycin D, cyclophosphamide, vincristine); high-risk (>8) receive EMA-CO, and if it fails, cisplatin/etoposide/bleomycin/ifosfamide.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The four triggers to give chemo — memorise these

>> **1. β-hCG > 20,000 IU/ml persisting 4 weeks after evacuation**
>> **2. Rising β-hCG**
>> **3. Choriocarcinoma on histology**
>> **4. Metastasis**

**The logic:** β-hCG is the **tumour marker** for trophoblastic tissue. If it's **very high, rising, or the tissue is frankly malignant or has spread**, the disease won't resolve on its own → **chemo.**

---

## Step 2: Why β-hCG is the perfect guide

Trophoblast makes β-hCG, so its level **tracks the amount of disease.** After a molar pregnancy is evacuated, β-hCG should **fall.** If it **plateaus high or rises**, viable tumour remains.

---

## Step 3: Score the risk (WHO)

Factors: age, antecedent pregnancy, **β-hCG level**, interval, tumour size, site and number of metastases, prior chemo — each scored 0–4.

>> The score sorts patients into **low, medium, high** risk, which picks the regimen.

---

## Step 4: Match regimen to risk — the key line

| Risk | Score | Regimen |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** | 0–4 | **Methotrexate** (± folinic acid) or actinomycin D — **single agent** |
| **Medium** | 5–7 | **EMA-CO** (combination) |
| **High** | > 8 | **EMA-CO**; if fails, cisplatin/etoposide/bleomycin/ifosfamide |

>> **The one line to remember: LOW risk → methotrexate; HIGHER risk → EMA-CO.**

---

## Step 5: Why GTD is special

>> **GTD is one of the most chemo-CURABLE cancers** — even metastatic disease is often cured, because trophoblast is exquisitely chemosensitive and β-hCG lets you monitor response precisely.

---

## Putting it all together

Give chemo in GTD for **β-hCG > 20,000 at 4 weeks, rising β-hCG, choriocarcinoma, or metastasis.** Score risk by the **WHO system**, then treat: **low risk → single-agent methotrexate; medium/high risk → EMA-CO combination.** GTD is highly chemo-curable, and **β-hCG both triggers and monitors treatment.**
cards.tie: GTD chemotherapy is a classic O&G exam question, and the four indications plus the methotrexate-versus-EMA-CO split are exactly what is tested. It is also clinically important because GTD is one of the most curable malignancies when treated correctly, with β-hCG as an ideal marker.

cards.q: KEY CUES: What words should immediately point you to a specific chemotherapy fact?
cards.lecturer: Key cues: tumour cells not faster but more dividing = the basis of chemo selectivity. First-order kinetics = kills a constant fraction, needs repeated cycles. Haematologic/marrow toxicity = most frequent side effect. Goldie-Coldman = resistance develops, so use combination therapy. Ovarian cancer = responds best, platinum + paclitaxel. Cervical cancer = chemoradiation (cisplatin +/- 5-FU). Vaginal cancer = chemo has no activity. BEP = ovarian germ-cell tumours. EMA-CO = medium/high risk GTD. Methotrexate = low-risk GTD. beta-hCG > 20,000 at 4 weeks / rising / choriocarcinoma / metastasis = indications for chemo in GTD. Cisplatin = nephrotoxic. Adriamycin = cardiotoxic. Bleomycin = pulmonary and skin. Methotrexate = hepatotoxic. Vincristine/bleomycin = neurotoxic. Yuzpe regimen = emergency contraception within 72 hours. Osteoporosis = most important menopausal hazard.
cards.explain:
## Group 1 — Principles

>> **Not faster, just more dividing** → basis of selectivity

>> **First-order kinetics** → constant fraction → **repeated cycles**

>> **Marrow/haematologic toxicity** → **most frequent** side effect

>> **Goldie-Coldman** → resistance → use **combination** therapy

---

## Group 2 — Which cancer, which drugs

>> **Ovarian** → responds **best**; **platinum + paclitaxel**

>> **Ovarian germ-cell** → **BEP** (bleomycin, etoposide, platinum)

>> **Cervix (squamous)** → **chemoradiation** (cisplatin ± 5-FU)

>> **Vagina** → chemo has **NO activity** (the exception)

>> **Vulva (advanced)** → mitomycin C + 5-FU + radiotherapy

---

## Group 3 — GTD

>> **β-hCG > 20,000 at 4 weeks / rising / choriocarcinoma / metastasis** → indications for chemo

>> **Low risk** → **methotrexate**; **medium/high** → **EMA-CO**

---

## Group 4 — Drug-specific toxicities (exam gold)

>> **Cisplatin → kidney**

>> **Adriamycin (doxorubicin) → heart**

>> **Bleomycin → lung (and skin)**

>> **Methotrexate → liver**

>> **Vincristine/bleomycin → nerves**

---

## Group 5 — Hormone therapy

>> **Yuzpe regimen** → emergency contraception within **72 h**

>> **Hot flushes → oestrogen**

>> **Osteoporosis** → the **most important menopausal hazard**

---

## Putting it all together

Anchor on: **combination therapy beats resistance**, **ovarian responds best / vagina not at all**, **GTD = methotrexate (low) or EMA-CO (higher)**, and the **drug-toxicity pairs** — cisplatin-kidney, adriamycin-heart, bleomycin-lung, methotrexate-liver, vincristine-nerves. These cover most of what is asked.
cards.tie: These cues are the shortcut to answering chemotherapy questions, which cluster around drug-specific toxicities, which cancer responds to what, and the GTD regimens. Recognising the cue tells you the answer immediately.
